description: First + strongest heuristic of the status-reconciliation pass (7625d5e6): an OPEN aim whose 8-char id prefix appears in >=1 git commit message is almost certainly already implemented (this repo's convention is to cite the aim id in the commit that advances it) but never got flipped to done. Read-only review report, ranked by commit count. Deterministic, cheap, high-precision — no embeddings/LLM needed.

reflections.context: Automate the open-but-already-implemented drift detection I keep doing by hand. Needed a cheap, high-precision first signal before reaching for embeddings/LLM.
reflections.outcome: Reused the existing realized-commit signal (getRepoCommitMessages + countAimReferences already powering get_prioritized_aims' realizedCommits) to ship a reconcile_status MCP tool: OPEN aims whose 8-char id prefix appears in a commit are surfaced, ranked by count. Extracted the pure logic into a new reconcile.ts so it's unit-testable without booting the MCP server / WS client.
reflections.effectiveness: Good leverage: the signal already existed and was proven; the only new code is a tiny pure selector + a thin tool handler + tests. Catching the side effect early mattered — tools.ts imports client.ts which opens a WS client at module load, so a test importing tools.ts would hang; moving the pure functions to a side-effect-free module sidestepped that (same reason the backend has duplicate-detection.ts).
reflections.lesson: Before adding a heuristic, check whether the signal is already computed somewhere — realizedCommits was right there in get_prioritized_aims; reconciliation is just a different VIEW of it (filter open + cite-count), not new machinery. And when a file pulls in a networked client at import, extract pure logic to its own module so tests don't drag the client in.

reflections.context: Dogfooded reconcile_status against the live graph immediately after building it (the running MCP server has the old build, so I ran the exact logic by hand: open-aim id-prefixes vs git log).
reflections.outcome: The raw heuristic flagged ~24 candidates, mostly garbage: vision goals (achieve ASI, Build the Cooperation Engine), structural parents, and even the two sub-aims my OWN reconcile commit had just cited as future work. Root cause: graph-bookkeeping commits (chore(graph)/chore(bowman) triage/reframe/handoff) cite many aim ids without implementing anything. Fix: count only commits that touched files outside .bowman/ (git pathspec ':(exclude).bowman'). That cut candidates 24→6 and surfaced the one genuine drift, 5af61b3e (self-rebuild loop), which I corrected open→partially.
reflections.effectiveness: High-value loop: build→dogfood→find precision flaw→fix→re-validate, all in one turn. Validating the pathspec fix with real git BEFORE coding it meant the change was known-good when committed. mcp tsc + 4 pure tests stayed green (the fix is in the impure git read; the pure selector is unchanged).
reflections.lesson: A commit MENTIONING an aim id is much weaker evidence than a commit IMPLEMENTING it — and a graph that records its own maintenance in commits will cite aim ids constantly. Filter the signal to code-touching commits, or the reconciliation drowns in its own bookkeeping. Residual false positives remain (a commit can cite an aim as future/related work; vision/parent aims get cited by their children's commits) — noted for the keyword/symbol (da1291e5) and leaf-vs-parent refinements.
reflections.pattern: dogfood-immediately; the signal that records bookkeeping must exclude bookkeeping.

status.comment: Done 2026-06-28 (commit pending). New reconcile_status MCP tool (packages/mcp/src/tools.ts) backed by a pure, side-effect-free packages/mcp/src/reconcile.ts (findReconciliationCandidates + countAimReferences moved there so they're testable without the MCP server/WS client). Lists OPEN aims referenced by >=1 commit (8-char prefix match), ranked by count, with a git-unavailable/no-candidates note. 4 unit tests (prefix counting; ranked surfacing; non-open excluded; empty). Verified: mcp tsc clean; reconcile tests 4/4.
